coredata.dc:title: PLGA-graphene quantum dot nanocomposites targeted against αvβ3 integrin receptor for sorafenib delivery in angiogenesis
coredata.dc:creator: Rahiminezhad, Zahra
Tamaddon, AliMohammad
Dehshahri, Ali
Borandeh, Sedigheh
Abolmaali, Samira Sadat
Najafi, Haniyeh
Azarpira, Negar
coredata.dc:description: Angiogenesis is a vital step in many severe diseases such as cancer, diabetic retinopathy, and rheumatoid arthritis. Sorafenib (SFB), a multi-tyrosine kinase inhibitor, has recently been shown to inhibit tumor progression and suppress angiogenesis. Its narrow therapeutic window, however, has limited its clinical application and therapeutic efficacy. Accordingly, in this study, a nanocomposite formulation comprising of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and poly (D, l-lactide-co-glycolide) (PLGA) nanoparticles was functionalized with an integrin-targeting ligand (RGD peptide) to improve SFB delivery for the treatment of angiogenesis. Physicochemical and biological properties of the targeted nanocomposite were evaluated in terms of chemical structure, morphology, particle size, zeta potential, photoluminescence, and cell toxicity. The loading capacity of the nanocomposite was optimized at different drug-to-PLGA ratios. Drug release behavior was also investigated at 37 °C in pH = 7.4. The SFB-to-PLGA ratio of 1:3 was selected as the optimum condition which resulted in the encapsulation efficiency and encapsulation capacity of 68.93 ± 1.39 and 18.77 ± 0.46, respectively. Photoluminescence properties of GQD in nanocomposite were used to track the delivery system. The results indicated that conjugating targeting ligand could enhance cellular uptake of nanocomposite in cells overexpressing integrin receptors. In vivo anti-angiogenesis activity of targeted nanocomposite was investigated in chick chorioallantoic membrane (CAM). The findings showed that SFB loaded in the targeted nanocomposite reduced VEGF secretion in vitro and its anti-angiogenic effect surpass free SFB. Thanks to its unique therapeutic and bioimaging properties, the developed nanocomposite could be an effective drug delivery system for poorly water-soluble therapeutic agents.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Angiogenesis
Sorafenib
PLGA
Graphene quantum dot
Targeted delivery

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the GQD-CMC-RGD-PLGA-targeted nanocomposite (GCRP-NC) synthesis.

(A) TGA curves for GQD, GQD-CMC-RGD, and GCRP-NC. (B) Photoluminescence emission spectra of GQD, GQD-CMC-RGD and GCRP-NC exited at λ = 440 nm. SEM images of (C) PLGA NPs and (D) GCRP-NCs. Inserts are the related TEM images (scale bare 50 nm).
Cumulative release profiles of SFB from the SFB-loaded PLGA-NPs and GCRP-NC at 37 °C and pH 7.4.

MTT assay of the cell viability after 24 h exposure to various concentrations of (A) GCRP-NC (B) free SFB and SFB-loaded GCRP-NC. Note: *P < 0.05; **P < 0.01; ***P < 0.001; ****P < 0.0001; NS, not significant.
In vitro fluorescence images of HUVECs incubated with GCRP-NC (RGD+), GCRP-NC (RGD−), and GCRP-NC (RGD+) in presence of excess free ligand.

Flow cytometry analysis of the cellular uptake of targeted nanocomposite NC (RGD+) and non-targeted nanocomposite NC (RGD−) in HUVECs (positive αvβ3 integrin receptor) and HepG2 cells (negative αvβ3 integrin receptor).

The CAM vasculature treated with PBS (control), NC (RGD+), NC (RGD−), SFB-loaded GCRP-NC, and free SFB on Day 14 for 3 days. (A) The stereomicroscope images of the CAM vessels and (B) quantitative analysis by NIH ImageJ with the angiogenesis analyzer plugin. Total vessels network length (Tot. Length), number of branching points (Nb. Branches), number of nods (Nb. Node), and number of junction points (Nb. Junctions). Data are expressed as mean ± SD (n = 6). Notes: *P < 0.05; **P < 0.01; ***P < 0.001; ****P < 0.0001; NS, not significant.
Particle size, zeta potential, encapsulation efficiency (EE %), and encapsulation capacity (EC %) of SFB-loaded PLGA-NPs in different ratios of SFB to PLGA.

head.ce:title: PLGA-graphene quantum dot nanocomposites targeted against αvβ3 integrin receptor for sorafenib delivery in angiogenesis

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pharmaceutical Nanotechnology Department, School of Pharmay, Shiraz University of Medical Sciences, Shiraz 71345, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Pharmaceutical Nanotechnology Department, School of Pharmay, Shiraz University of Medical Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center for Nanotechnology in Drug Delivery, Shiraz University of Medical Sciences, Shiraz 71345, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Pharmaceutical Nanotechnology Department and Center for Nanotechnology in Drug Delivery, Shiraz University of Medical Sciences, Shiraz 71345, Iran.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Pharmaceutical Nanotechnology Department and Center for Nanotechnology in Drug Delivery

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Angiogenesis is a vital step in many severe diseases such as cancer, diabetic retinopathy, and rheumatoid arthritis. Sorafenib (SFB), a multi-tyrosine kinase inhibitor, has recently been shown to inhibit tumor progression and suppress angiogenesis. Its narrow therapeutic window, however, has limited its clinical application and therapeutic efficacy. Accordingly, in this study, a nanocomposite formulation comprising of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and poly (D, l-lactide-co-glycolide) (PLGA) nanoparticles was functionalized with an integrin-targeting ligand (RGD peptide) to improve SFB delivery for the treatment of angiogenesis. Physicochemical and biological properties of the targeted nanocomposite were evaluated in terms of chemical structure, morphology, particle size, zeta potential, photoluminescence, and cell toxicity. The loading capacity of the nanocomposite was optimized at different drug-to-PLGA ratios. Drug release behavior was also investigated at 37 °C in pH = 7.4. The SFB-to-PLGA ratio of 1:3 was selected as the optimum condition which resulted in the encapsulation efficiency and encapsulation capacity of 68.93 ± 1.39 and 18.77 ± 0.46, respectively. Photoluminescence properties of GQD in nanocomposite were used to track the delivery system. The results indicated that conjugating targeting ligand could enhance cellular uptake of nanocomposite in cells overexpressing integrin receptors. In vivo anti-angiogenesis activity of targeted nanocomposite was investigated in chick chorioallantoic membrane (CAM). The findings showed that SFB loaded in the targeted nanocomposite reduced VEGF secretion in vitro and its anti-angiogenic effect surpass free SFB. Thanks to its unique therapeutic and bioimaging properties, the developed nanocomposite could be an effective drug delivery system for poorly water-soluble therapeutic agents.

Integrin receptor-mediated delivery of sorafenib by PLGA-graphene quantum dot nanocomposites in angiogenesis.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Angiogenesis is a greatly regulated biological process that eventuates in the development of new blood vessels from existing ones. This crucial process is involved not only in growth and development, but also in many serious diseases such as cancer, diabetic retinopathy, and rheumatoid arthritis [1]. Tumor angiogenesis is an important pathologic feature of cancer that promotes tumor growth and metastasis by supplying oxygen and nutrients required for the proliferation of tumor cells [2]. Tumor angiogenesis inhibition, along with chemotherapy, surgery, and radiotherapy, has become a clinical anticancer approach in recent years, emphasizing the critical role of angiogenesis in the early stages of tumor growth.

Sorafenib (SFB) is a multi-tyrosine kinase inhibitor [3] capable of suppressing angiogenesis and tumor progression [4,5]. However, its oral administration leads to a variety of side effects such as diarrhea, hand-foot syndrome, and hypertension due to non-specific disposition in common tissues [6]. Furthermore, its low aqueous solubility limits absorption through the gastrointestinal tract, resulting in poor pharmacokinetics and a narrow therapeutic window [7]. Thus, a targeted strategy for increasing SFB availability in tumor tissue is highly required for improving therapeutic efficacy and reducing toxicity.

The integrin receptor αvβ3 is involved in many biological processes, such as tumor metastasis and angiogenesis [8,9]. It is overexpressed in a variety of cancers, including thyroid cancer, lung carcinoma, and breast tumors, and especially on tumor tissue vessels [10–12]. Furthermore, the integrin receptors can improve adenovirus internalization [13–15]. These receptors are known as vitronectin receptors as they can bind to extracellular environment ligands and connect the extracellular matrix to intracellular signaling pathways, thereby enhancing cell entry [16]. Therefore, targeting integrin receptors in tumor tissue could be a beneficial strategy to enhance cell delivery and internalization via receptor-mediated pathways, while minimizing the side effects associated with systemic administration of chemotherapeutics [17]. The arginine-glycine-aspartate tripeptide (Arg-Gly-Asp, RGD) has a high affinity for the αvβ3 integrin receptor, which is widely expressed in new vascular endothelial cells, causing apoptosis and the death of tumor neo-vessels [18]. Furthermore, it has been shown that targeted drug delivery systems with specific ligands improve their delivery to cancer cells. More specifically, using RGD peptide which interacts selectively with αvβ3 integrin overexpressed on tumor endothelial cells, resulted in preferential drug transfer to cancer cells and improved tumor therapy [19,20]. Hence, RGD is used as a targeting ligand in the design of a targeted delivery system against the αvβ3 integrin receptor in angiogenesis.

FDA-approved Poly (D, l-lactide-co-glycolide) (PLGA) provides a biodegradable and biocompatible nanoparticle that can be used in drug delivery systems for in vivo administration [21]. PLGA nanoparticles (NPs) have been extensively studied for loading numerous chemotherapeutic drugs such as cisplatin [22], fluorouracil [23], doxorubicin [24], Epirubicin [25], and docetaxel [26]. For example, SFB-loaded PEG-modified PLGA NPs [27] have been shown to prolong blood circulation, improve uptake, and normalize vessel density in fibrotic livers. Furthermore, PLGA NPs conjugated with a monoclonal antibody (cetuximab) were employed for targeted delivery of SFB to undifferentiated/anaplastic thyroid carcinoma cells [28]. The results showed a higher specificity of the PLGA-NPs decorated with cetuximab in the anaplastic cells line overexpressing the epithelial growth factor receptor than in the normal thyroid cell line. This study suggests that PLGA NPs could be used for targeted SFB delivery.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have recently received a huge deal of attention due to their fascinating properties, such as small size, chemical inertness, photoluminescence stability, electronic properties, and good compatibility and low toxicity with biological systems [29]. Thanks to their versatile optical properties, GQDs could be used as optical sensing probes for biological imaging. GQDs have also shown promising drug delivery potential due to their large specific surface area with different surface functionalities which can be applied for simultaneous drug delivery carrier and fluorescent imaging and monitoring in cancer treatment [30]. For example, GQD was used as a capping agent in the preparation of GQDs-gated hollow mesoporous carbon nanoparticles for targeting delivery and chemo-photothermal therapy [31] which resulted in controlled drug release, induced hyperthermia, and improved therapeutic efficacy of anticancer therapy.

PLGA and GQDs have distinct properties and a variety of intriguing applications for the development of a multi-functional drug delivery system. The present study is aimed to develop an innovative SFB delivery system for simultaneous imaging, tracking, and targeting angiogenesis through simultaneous exploitation of the fascinating photoluminescence properties of GQDs, biodegradability and biocompatibility of PLGA, and the αvβ3 integrin receptor recognition ability of RGD peptide. In this research, fluorescent GQDs were synthesized and conjugated with RGD, using carboxymethyl chitosan (CMC) as a linker for a specific cell imaging and tracking system. GQD-CMC-RGD was then conjugated to the surface of PLGA nanoparticles loaded with SFB to develop the GQD-CMC-RGD-PLGA-targeted nanocomposite (GCRP-NC) to be used in the targeted delivery of SFB in the treatment of angiogenesis (Fig. 1 ).

Graphite powder (particle size = 70 μm, purity = 99.999%) was obtained from Merck Chemical Company (Germany). N-hydroxy succinimide (NHS), Potassium permanganate (KMnO4), sodium carbonate (Na2CO3), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), sulfuric acid (H2SO4), nitric acid (HNO3), 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), were all prepared from Sigma-Aldrich Inc. (St. Louis, MO, USA). Carboxy methyl chitosan (CMC) from ChemCruz Biochemicals, (Dallas, TX, USA). PLGA-COOH terminated (50/50 DL-lactide/glycolide copolymer) was obtained from Corbion biomaterials and RGD was prepared from Abcam (UK).

GQDs and the PLGA nanocomposite were characterized by the following techniques: UV–vis spectra were obtained on a UV–vis spectrophotometer (PowerWave-XS, BioTeK, USA). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tecan, Austria). The GQDs' morphology and size were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Zeiss - EM10C - 100 kV). The functional groups of GQDs were assessed using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (Vertex, Bruker, Germany). Raman spectrum of the synthesized GQD was gained using Raman spectrometer (a Lab Ram HR confocal Raman, Horiba, Japan). The Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) spectroscopy was obtained by NMR spectrometers (Bruker-400 MHz, German). Fluorescence images were attained using a fluorescence microscopy (Eclipse E-600Carl Zeiss, Jena, Germany). The hydrodynamic diameter and polydispersity of nanocomposite were determined by dynamic light scattering (DLS 180°, Nano-flex, Particle Metrics GmbH, Germany) in phosphate buffer (25 mM, pH 7.4). Zeta-potential was measured in phosphate buffer (10 mM, pH 7.4) at 25 °C (ZETA-check, Colloid Metrics GmbH, Germany). High performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) was applied for SFB assay by HPLC unit (Knauer, Germany) with C18 (Knauer, Germany) column. Volume injection was 100 μL by manual syringe. The mobile phase was contained methanol and water (80:20, v/v) and the flow rate was set at 1.2 mL/min. Quantification was performed at 260 nm. All the reagents used were HPLC grade. The geometry of the nanocomposite was observed by the scanning electron microscope (TESCAN-Vega 3). Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed under nitrogen atmosphere that the heating rate was set at 10 °C/min from ambient temperature to 800°C (STA504 win TA, Bahr-Thermoanalyse GmbH, Hüllhorst, Germany).

GQDs were synthesized from graphite powder via a one-step hydrothermal reaction [32]. For this aim, a mixture of graphite (60 mg) and potassium permanganate (180 mg) were added into the 5:1 ratio H2SO4/HNO3 mixture (18 mL). The mixed solution was moved in Teflon autoclave reactor (60 mL) and heated at 160 °C for 2 h. After the reaction, unreacted potassium permanganate was neutralized by adding hydrogen peroxide solution and unreacted acids were neutralized by adding Na2CO3 saturated solution. In the following, the reaction was dialyzed for 2 day (cut-off of 2000 Da) (Fig. S1). TEM, TGA, FT-IR spectroscopy, Raman spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, and zeta potential were applied for GQD characterization, as the related article also used [33,34].

The RGD peptide was conjugated with CMC as a linker through amide bond formation between amino group-terminated RGD and carboxyl group-containing CMC using EDC and NHS as coupling agents. In brief, EDC (4 mg/25.8 μmol) and NHS (6 mg/52.1 μmol) were mixed into a PBS solution of CMC (1 mg/mL). After 1 h, RGD (1 mg/mL solution in PBS) was added to the reaction and stirred at 25 °C. The product was then purified by dialysis (cut-off 2000 Da) for 48 h, and RGD-CMC conjugate was obtained by lyophilization.

For synthesis of RGD-CMC, 10 mg of GQD in 10 mL of PBS (pH = 7.4) was sonicated for 10 min to properly disperse before being modified with RGD-CMC. EDC (9 mg) and NHS (12 mg) were then added into the GQD dispersion under magnetic stirring to activate the carboxyl groups of the GQD. After 2 h, a CMC-RGD solution (1 mg/mL) was added to the mixture, which was stirred at 25 °C overnight. The excess reactants and byproducts were then removed by extensive dialysis, and lyophilization was eventually used to achieve GQD-CMC-RGD.

PLGA NPs were prepared through emulsion-solvent evaporation [35]. The organic phase consisted of 25 mg PLGA-COOH and 50 mg poloxamer 80 dissolved in 5 mL acetone as organic phase. The organic phase was then dropped into 35 mL deionized water (DI water) with vigorous magnetic stirring, and the mixture was homogenized for 3 min at 10,000 rpm to obtain an o/w emulsion. The result emulsion was then stirred for another 5 h at 50 °C. Therefore, the organic solvent was completely evaporated and PLGA NPs were formed. After that, the PLGA nanoparticles were centrifugated for 20 min (14,000 rpm), collected, and washed three times in DI water.

Prepared PLGA-NPs with carboxylic acid functionality were covalently conjugated to amine groups of CMC in GQD-CMC-RGD. In this regard, EDC (12 mg) and NHS (18 mg) were added to a dispersion of PLGA-NPs (1 mg/mL) in PBS (pH = 7.4) and stirred for 2 h. GQD-CMC-RGD (1 mg/mL) was added to the reaction mixture and allowed to proceed for 18 h. Extensive dialysis was used to remove the excess reactants. Finally, lyophilization was used to obtain GCRP-NC. We also used all accessible techniques for GCRP-NC characterization including FT-IR spectroscopy, SEM, TEM, TGA, DLS, and zeta potential [36].

SFB-loaded PLGA NPs were prepared as described in the Section 2.6. SFB and PLGA polymer were dissolved in 5 mL acetone as the organic phase at 4 different ratios (SFB to PLGA: 1/2, 1/3, 1/5, 1/10 w/w). The organic phase was then added dropwise to the DI water while stirring vigorously, and the mixture was homogenized to obtain o/w emulsion (12,000 rpm, 3 min). The rest were carried out in accordance with Section 2.6. The supernatant was collected and analyzed by HPLC for the non-encapsulated (free) drug to determine the drug loading. The encapsulation capacity and encapsulation efficiency were then calculated by the following equations:

SFB-loaded nanocomposite release behavior was investigated in PBS at pH 7.4 and 37 °C. Briefly, SFB-loaded particles (0.3 mg) were placed in centrifuge microtubes (triplicate) and dispersed in 1 mL of release medium containing 1% Tween 80. After incubation for the specified times (0.5, 1, 2, 3, 8, 24, 48, and 120 h), microtubes were centrifuged (15,000 rpm, 20 min), and 300 μL of supernatant was taken out and 300 μL of fresh medium was replaced. The pellet was resuspended, and microtubes put back to the shaker incubator (140 rpm at 37 °C). The amount of SFB that was released from NPs, was then determined via the HPLC method described in Section 2.2.

The Human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVECs) were cultured in DMEM medium, supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum and 1% penicillin and streptomycin. Cells were incubated in at 37 °C in a humidified 5% CO2. Passage would be carried out when the cells reached confluency.

In-vitro cytotoxicity was evaluated by MTT assay against HUVEC cells using a standard protocol [46]. The cells were grown in 96-well plates (10 × 104 cells per well) and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. After 24 h, the culture medium was removed and 100 μL of GQD, GCRP-NC, SFB-loaded GCRP-NC, and SFB in culture medium was replaced at various concentrations for 24 h. The culture medium was then exchanged the MTT reagent (0.5 mg/mL), and incubated for 4 h at 37 °C. After removing the medium, 100 μL of DMSO was immediately added to each well. The purple formazan crystals were solubilized in DMSO, and their color absorbance was then read at λ = 570 nm by a microplate reader (BioTek, USA). The cell viability percent was calculated with reference λ = 650 nm for control group.

Fluorescence microscopy was used to assess cellular uptake of GCRP-NC. The photoluminescence properties of GQD were used as a fluorescence probe to track the synthesized nanocomposite (λexit = 430 nm and λemit = 520 nm, which can be visualized through a FITC filter). In 6-well plates, 1 × 105 HUVEC cells were seeded and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C. The culture medium was then exchanged with 1 mL of NC containing RGD (NC (RGD+)) and NC without RGD (NC (RGD−)) for 4 h. The binding inhibition assay was performed using excess free RGD ligand to demonstrate the impact of RGD conjugation on improving cellular uptake of NC via integrin receptors. To accomplish this, the HUVECs were first pre-treated for 30 min at 37 °C with the free ligand (10−5 mol/mL). The GCRP-NC (RGD+) was then added to the medium and incubated for 4 h. Similarly, the treated cells were washed by PBS and fixed using 2% paraformaldehyde solution in PBS for 10 min at 37 °C. The cells were then washed with cold PBS after being incubated with 0.5% Triton X-100 solution in PBS for 10 min at 4 °C. As a final step, the nuclei of the cells were stained with DAPI solution in PBS (10 μg/mL) for 5 min at 37 °C, and the NC uptake was detected using fluorescence microscopy (CKX53, Olympus, Japan).

The cellular uptake of NC via integrin receptors was quantitatively investigated by flow cytometry assay. For this purpose, 1 × 105 of HUVEC cells (as positive αvβ3 integrin receptor) and HepG2 cells (as negative αvβ3 integrin receptor) were cultured in 6-well plates and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C. Afterward, the culture medium was removed, and cells were incubated for 4 h with 1 mL of NC (RGD+) and NC (RGD). To examine the impact of RGD conjugation on cellular uptake of NC via integrin receptors, HUVEC cells were pre-treated with the free ligand (RGD) (30 min, 37 °C). The NC (RGD+) was then added to the cell medium and incubated for another 4 h. The cells were washed with PBS, trypsinized, and flow cytometry analysis was performed using FITC emission filter (Becton-Dickinson, USA).

For this purpose, 1 × 105 of HUVEC cells were cultured in 6-well plates and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C. After HUVECs seeding, they were incubated with various samples including NC (RGD+), NC (RGD), SFB-loaded-NC, and SFB (1 μM) for 24 h. HUVECs were collected, and VEGF levels in the supernatant of HUVECs were measured using an ELISA kit (ZellBio GmbH, Germany) according to the manufacturer's instructions.

Chick chorioallantoic membrane (CAM) model assay was used to evaluate the NC's anti-angiogenic activity. At the first embryonic development day, 25 fertilized eggs were put in an upright position and incubated at 37 °C in 70% humidity. On the ninth day, under aseptic condition, a small hole was made in the bottom of the eggshell under aseptic condition. PBS (as a control), NC (RGD+), NC (RGD−), SFB-loaded GCRP-NC, and free SFB were all applied on top of CAM. The window was then closed, and the eggs were incubated for another three days at 37 °C in 70% humidity. On twelfth day of the chicken embryo development, CAM were removed and photographed under stereomicroscope. The pictures were analyzed by NIH ImageJ using the Angiogenesis plugin.

Result data were presented as the mean ± SD (n). Statistical analysis was achieved using Prism 8 Software. Significance was determined using t-tests or one-way ANOVA, and p values ≤ 0.05 were considered significant.

The desired GQD-CMC-PLGA-RGD nanocomposite was constructed using hydrothermally synthesized GQDs from graphite powder (G). The carboxylic acid on the surface of GQDs was then grafted to the developed CMC-RGD conjugate, which also serves as a linker for conjugating to PLGA-NPs (Fig. 1). Thanks to its biocompatibility and photoluminescence properties [37], the GQD component adds the ability to track the delivery system within the cells. CMC was employed as a hydrophilic linker. It has both amine (NH2) and carboxylic acid (CO2H) functionalities and can be grafted to GQDs, RGDs, and PLGA-NPs through amide bond formation. In addition to improving biocompatibility [38], CMC also brings desired components into one unit, resulting in the multifunctional nanocomposite. PLGA, an FDA-approved biodegradable hydrophobic copolymer [39], was chosen as a drug loading component in the construction of nanocomposite (GCRP-NC). The nanocomposite was further functionalized with RGD peptides as a targeting moiety, which could increase cellular uptake by αvβ3 integrin receptor [40,41].

Fig. S1 demonstrates a schematic illustration of the GQDs synthesis method. TEM image (Fig. S2.A) shows that the fabricated GQDs possess a fairly narrow size distribution (from 3.0 to 7.5 nm, with an average diameter of 5 nm). FTIR spectroscopy was carried out to characterize the functional groups at the edges of GQDs. In comparison to graphite, GQDs contain diverse chemical groups, including OH (3427 cm−1), CO (1130 cm−1), and CO (1723 cm−1) (Fig. S2.B). These functional groups were formed on the edges of GQDs during the oxidative cutting process. Therefore, the resulting GQDs had carboxylic acid groups, which increased their water solubility. Furthermore, these functional groups at the edges of GQDs facilitate further GQD modification and can be conjugated with biocompatible polymers. Raman spectroscopy was also carried out to characterize the GQDs. As shown in Fig. S2.C, the Raman spectrum indicated a D band and a G band associated with sp 3-like disordered carbon and ordered sp 2 bonded carbon, respectively. The Raman spectrum of GQD was divided into two distinctive bands ~1342 cm−1 (D bond) and ~1588 cm−1 (G band), whereas those of graphite were at ~1346 cm−1 (D bond) and ~1573 cm−1 (G bond). For the graphite, the crystalline grains are known to be inversely relative to the intensity ratio of (ID/IG) [42]. The ID/IG of graphite was 0.305 while the ID/IG of GQDs was increased to 1.015. This result confirms that graphite has a G-band-dominated structure, whereas GQDs have a D-band dominance structure, suggesting an sp 3-type disorder in GQD as well as its greater defective structure compared to graphite sheets [43]. Due to sp 3-like disorders in GQDs structures, unsaturated carbon atoms in the hexagonal geometry network form carboxylate groups with oxygen atoms, which causes an increment in the intensity of the D band. All of these findings support the existence of functional groups at the edges of GQDs as previously confirmed in FTIR results. Similar to most luminescent carbon-based NPs, GQDs exhibit excitation-dependent photoluminescence (PL), with emission peaks shifting by varying excitation wavelengths [37]. When excited at 440 nm, the GQD dispersion exhibits a strong emission peak at 510 nm, indicating the size uniformity of the GQDs (Fig. S2.D).

According to Fig. 1, the RGD peptide was conjugated with CMC via an EDC/NHS coupling reaction between the CMC carboxyl groups and the amine-terminated groups of RGD peptide. Fig. S3 shows the FT-IR spectra of CMC and CMC-RGD conjugation. The FTIR spectrum of CMC revealed bands at 3430 cm−1 which can be assigned to OH and NH2 as well as a band at 2897 cm−1 attributable to the CH and CH2 bonds in the polymer chain. Moreover, the peaks at 1720, 1532, and 1080 cm−1 can be ascribed to the carbonyl stretching vibration in the carboxylic acid, bending vibration of NH bond of the primary amine, and the CO bond. The CMC-RGD conjugate presented several changes. The carboxylate-associated peaks faded, while the amide-associated peaks (1638 cm−1) became stronger. Furthermore, CMC-corresponding stretching vibration of the CH and CO bonds at 2897 and 1080 cm−1 were also observed in the CMC-RGD spectrum (Fig. S3.A). Moreover, the 1H NMR analysis was performed on CMC-RGD conjugate and compared with CMC and RGD results (Fig. S3.B–D). No CMC carboxylate-associated peak (δ: 8.05) was observed in the 1H NMR spectrum of the CMC-RGD, the emergence of amide-associated peaks (δ: 5.62–5.88 (m, NH)) indicated that the RGD was successfully grafted to CMC.

The CMC-RGD conjugate was then used to modify the surface of GQDs and develop a delivery system to target vascular endothelial cells via interaction with the αvβ3 integrin receptor. The GQD was conjugated with CMC-RGD through the EDC/NHS coupling reaction between the carboxyl groups of GQD and amine groups of CMC. FTIR spectroscopy was utilized to characterize the GQD surface functionalization. After grafting CMC-RGD, the intensity of the OH vibration bond (3400 cm−1) decreased in GQD. A peak also appeared at 2889 cm−1 corresponding to the CH2 stretching vibration of CMC (Fig. S3.A). Simultaneously, the formation of amide bonds in the GQD-CMC-RGD was confirmed with high-intensity bands at 1540 and 1643 cm−1 attributable to the NH bending vibration and CO stretching vibration, respectively. Meanwhile, the band at 1727 cm−1 (associated with the carboxylic acid carbonyl of GQDs) got weaker, indicating the involvement of this group in the formation of amide bonds. Apart from FTIR spectroscopy, the hydrodynamic size of GQDs was increased to 36.1 nm in the GQD-CMC-RGD, confirming conjugation of GQDs to CMC-RGD [44]. The GQD-CMC-RGD was well dispersed and exhibited a narrow size distribution with no aggregation (Table 1 ). Besides, GQDs were found to be negatively charged based on the zeta-potential measurements (−41 mV). Regarding the formation of the amide bond and the cationic nature of NH2 on CMC, the zeta potential was reduced to −27 mV after grafting GQDs to the CMC-RGD conjugate.

Based on previous studies, emulsion-solvent evaporation is the most common method for the preparation of PLGA NPs. Using this approach, the hydrophobic drugs can be encapsulated into the NPs. The final particle size can be affected by altering the ratio of the organic to the aqueous phase, the water miscibility of the organic phase, the stirring rate, and using high-speed homogenization or sonication [45]. For targeted drug delivery, the particle size below 200 nm is favorable to exploit the EPR effect so a major fraction of particles can reach the target site [46,47].

In the present study, PLGA-NPs were fabricated using emulsion-solvent evaporation. By optimizing the above-mentioned parameters, the PLGA NPs with desired size (<100 nm) was achieved. SEM analysis confirmed the spherical morphology of the PLGA-NPs with particle sizes of approximately 70 nm (Fig. 2C). The DLS results also confirmed the synthesis of PLGA-NPs with a narrow particle size distribution. They also possessed a negative zeta-potential (Table 1), further promoting their stability in the following lyophilization.

The GQD-CMC-RGD amino groups were then conjugated with the carboxylic acids of PLGA-NPs to form the nanoconjugate (GCRP-NC). The FTIR spectrum of PLGA-NPs revealed the following main bands (Fig. S3.A): a broad strong band at around 3424 cm−1 (OH stretching); a weak band at 2880 cm−1 (CH2 stretching); a peak at 1739 cm−1 (ester carbonyl stretching); two moderate bonds at 1455 and 1262 cm−1 (CHCH3 and CH3 bending vibration, respectively); and one peak at 1093 cm−1 (esteric CO stretching). The FTIR spectrum of PLGA-NPs conjugated to GQD-CMC-RGD demonstrated a broad and strong band at about 3430 cm−1 associated with the OH vibration. The bond associated with the ester carbonyl group of the PLGA chain can be observed at 1730 cm−1. Meanwhile, the bands at 2920 cm−1 (CH2 stretching) and 1083 cm−1 (CO stretching) showed increased intensity, indicating the presence of PLGA in the nanocomposite structure (Fig. S3.A). Simultaneously, the amide bands were detected at 1531 cm−1 (NH bending vibration) and 1645 cm−1 (CO amide stretching vibration). TEM image (Fig. 2D) demonstrates a spherical GQD-CMC-RGD-PLGA nanocomposite (GCRP-NC) with a rough surface. The SEM image also revealed a few particle duplets within a uniform and discrete particle population (Fig. 2D). Based on DLS results (Table S1), after conjugating GQD-CMC-RGD to the PLGA NPs, the average particle size showed an increment. Furthermore, the zeta potential was reduced due to the involvement of the PLGA carboxylic acid in the formation of amide bonds with GQD-CMC-RGD.

TGA was also utilized to investigate the conjugation of GQD to CMC-RGD and the subsequent conjugation of the GQD-CMC-RGD to PLGA-NPs (Fig. 2A). TGA curves of pure graphite showed no weight loss between 25 and 800 °C [48]. The percentages of weight loss were ~73% and 88% for GQD-CMC-RGD and GCRP-NC, respectively. The TGA curves of GQD-CMC-RGD and GCRP-NC showed an increase in weight loss, which can be attributed to the increased content of organic components on the surface of GQD. Consistent with the results of other analyses, the significant difference in TGA curves confirmed the extensive modifications of GQDs.

The photoluminescence properties of GQDs, GQDs-CMC-RGD, and GCRP-NC were also examined (Fig. 2B). The resulting nanocomposite exhibited strong green photoluminescence, indicating successful conjugation of GQD-CMC-RGD to PLGA-NPs. When excited at 440 nm, they similarly exhibited an emission peak at 510 nm. The fluorescence of GQD was slightly reduced after conjugation with CMC and PLGA-NPs, indicating the slight influence of surface modifications on the photoluminescence property of GQDs [33]. The photoluminescence property of the synthesized nanocomposite was then used for tracking in the cellular study.

PLGA components can serve as a lipophilic reservoir for high-capacity SFB loading. Accordingly, SFB-loaded PLGA-NPs were prepared in four different SFB/PLGA ratios using the emulsion-solvent evaporation method. Table 1 shows a statistically insignificant increase in the hydrodynamic size of blank-PLGA-NPs (63 ± 19) compared to SFB-loaded PLGA-NPs (79 ± 20 nm). The SFB was loaded in PLGA NPs through hydrophobic interaction. Therefore, SFB loading may enhance the PLGA NP's hydrophobicity. Therefore, the PLGA NPs can be packed more tightly and expel their water content, consequently, their particle size did not significantly increase [49]. Furthermore, the zeta potentials changed from −33 for unloaded PLGA-NPs to −29 mV for the SFB-loaded one (SFB/PLGA 1:10). This may be due to the increase in the polymer ratio which enhanced the SFB encapsulation efficiency. The amine functional groups of the loaded SFB could neutralize the negative charges of the PLGA polymer, declining the absolute value of zeta potential [49]. A negative Zeta-potential greater than −25 mV indicates enough repulsion force between PLGA NPs to sustain stability in an aqueous medium.

As shown in Table 1, SFB encapsulation efficiency was significantly affected by the drug/polymer ratio. SFB was not efficiently loaded at the SFB/PLGA ratio of 1:2. By enhancing the polymer content, however, encapsulation efficiency (EE) was incremented to 91% at the SFB/PLGA ratio of 1:10. In contrast, a rise in the polymer ratio declined the loading percent. As the polymer ratio decreased from 1:10 to 1:3, the loading capacity (EC) increased. However, EC increment from 1:3 to 1:2 ratio was not significant. Such a trend can be due to the increased polymer ratio which enhanced hydrophobic space for carrying more amount of hydrophobic SFB. As a result, EE enhanced from 61.9% (in the 1:2 SFB/polymer ratio) to 91.0% (in 1:10 SFB/polymer ratio). Similar reports can be found in the literature [49] which expressed that SFB loading efficiency was significantly affected by the drug to polymer ratio. At low SFB/polymer ratios, the loading efficiency was low which rose by increasing the polymer. Wei et al. also observed that an increment in hydrophobic content of the NPs structure leads to more sites for hydrophobic interaction to encapsulate higher amounts of drug and enhance the drug-carrying efficiency [50]. Taking all into consideration, the SFB/PLGA ratio of 1:3 was selected for the subsequent in vitro and in vivo studies.

SFB release from PLGA-NPs and the GCRP-NC was assessed under physiological conditions (at 37 °C, pH of 7.4). The drug release was similarly less than 10% during the first hours, and approximately 25% of the loaded SFB was released after 8 h. Similar to other studies, a sustained drug release profile was achieved [27]. As reported in other studies, the prolongation of SFB release time can be attributed to the slow degradation of PLGA NPs. The drug release rate from PLGA NPs depends on drug diffusion from the surface of PLGA NPs and bulk erosion or swelling of PLGA polymer [51,52]. Fig. 3 illustrated that the SFB release from GCRP-NC became slower than from PLGA-NPs, possibly because of GCRP-NC degradation at a different rate (compared to PLGA-NPs) [27]. High SFB encapsulation efficiency and slow SFB release were also reported for micelle and liposomal formulations [53,54].

The in vitro cytotoxicity of the synthesized GCRP-NC, SFB-loaded GCRP-NC, and free SFB was assessed on HUVECs. After 24 h of incubation, no significant change was detected in cell viability at concentrations as high as 100 μg/mL for the drug-unloaded GCRP-NC (Fig. 4A). Even though the cell viability decreased at higher concentrations up to 500 μg/mL, more than 80% of the HUVECs survived. Therefore, the concentration used in the tracking and bioimaging experiments (100 μg/mL) was within a safe and biocompatible range. In the same way, Jichuan Qiu et al. used GQD as fluorescent carriers for anticancer drug delivery [40]. They also indicated that the use of GQD (up to 200 μg/mL) had no significant effect on the viability of both DU-145 and PC-3 carcinoma cell lines. These results demonstrate that the good biocompatibility GQDs along with their proper optical properties make them ideal candidates for biomedical purposes.

MTT assay was also used to compare the in vitro cytotoxicity of SFB-loaded GCRP-NC with that of free SFB (Fig. 4B). SFB alone was cytotoxic to HUVECs, whereas the SFB-loaded GCRP-NC exhibited lower but comparable cytotoxicity under the same conditions. Consistent with other reports on HUVECs [27], the Inhibitory concentration 50% (IC50) values for SFB-loaded GCRP-NC and free SFB in HUVECs were 1.58 and 1.13 (μM), respectively. As previous studies reported, encapsulation of SFB in PLGA NPs showed less toxic effect than free SFB that highlight the potential use of these NPs for minimizing the SFB side effects [27,28]. The lower cytotoxicity of SFB-loaded GCRP-NC compared to free SFB could be due to the sustained drug release from GCRP-NC [27]. SFB could be released into the extracellular medium and then diffused into the cells, or SFB-loaded GCRP-NC could enter the cells via a receptor-mediated pathway and release its content intracellularly [9].

Fluorescence microscopy was used to monitor the cellular uptake of the synthesized GCRP-NC in HUVECs overexpressing αvβ3 integrin receptors. The green fluorescence of GQD was used to track the cellular uptake of GCRP-NC. Fig. 5 shows the fluorescence image of HUVECs after 4 h of incubation with NC (RGD+), NC (RGD−), and NC (RGD+) at the presence of excess free RGD ligand. Green fluorescence was observed in all treatment groups, indicating the possibility of tracking GCRP-NC within cells. The green fluorescence intensity of NC (RGD+)-treated cells, on the other hand, was inevitably higher than NC (RGD−)-treated cells. This finding demonstrates the remarkable targetability of RGD peptides, which resulted in efficient cellular uptake. To confirm the role of RGD conjugation in improving cellular uptake efficiency via integrin receptors, HUVECs were incubated with excess free RGD before treatment with NC (RGD+). The resulting image showed that the NC (RGD+) + free RGD treatment presented lower green fluorescence compared to NC (RGD+). These findings supported the role of RGD in the specific cellular uptake of targeted NC (RGD+), as similarly reported in the literature [50]. For example, RGD peptides were conjugated to the GQD surface to target cancer cells overexpressing αvβ3 integrin receptor αvβ3 for doxorubicin (Dox) delivery [40]. The results indicate a more lethal effect for Dox-GQDs (RGD+) than Dox-GQDs (RGD−), which can be attributed to the higher cellular uptake provided by RGD to the Dox-GQD (RGD+), confirming the efficacy of RGD to locally accumulate nano-delivery system within cancer cells [9].

Flow cytometry was also employed to compare the cellular uptake of HUVECs to HepG2 cells to demonstrate specific targeting of the αvβ3 integrin receptor by NC (RGD+). Since HepG2 cells lack αvβ3 integrin receptors, the targeted NC (RGD+) showed no significant cellular uptake. Furthermore, the cellular uptake of GCRP-NC (RGD+) and GCRP-NC (RGD−) was comparable in HepG2 cells (Fig. 6 ). Interestingly, the NC (RGD+) increased the cellular internalization by about 7 folds in HUVECs as compared to HepG2 cells (Fig. 6). Compared to non-targeted NC (RGD−) and targeted NC (RGD+) + free RGD treatments, the targeted NC (RGD+) were significantly accumulated in HUVECs (p < 0.05). Therefore, flow cytometry analysis confirmed GCRP-NC (RGD+) as a successful approach for increasing the efficiency and specificity of cellular uptake by HUVECs, which was consistent with fluorescence microscopy findings.

Angiogenesis is a complex process with a vital role in tumor growth, invasion, and cancer metastasis. A key regulating factor in tumor angiogenesis is the vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) signaling pathway [55,56] that is essentially needed to stimulate the proliferation and the migration of endothelial cells and vessel formation from pre-existing blood vessels. VEGF is undoubtedly an effective target for tumor angiogenesis prevention and control [57]. VEGF receptor (VEGFR) is one of the SFB-targeted pathways that has been validated as an anti-angiogenic therapy for the clinical treatment of metastatic renal cancer and hepatocellular carcinoma [58,59]. Therefore, we investigated the effect of GCRP-NC, either SFB-loaded or unloaded, on the concentration of VEGF in HUVEC supernatant using an ELISA kit. The concentration of VEGF in the supernatant of HUVECs treated with free SFB, SFB-loaded NC (RGD−), and SFB-loaded NC (RGD+) was significantly reduced from the baseline (untreated control,236.04 pg/mL) to 130.41, 120.12, and 66.23, respectively. Unlike SFB-loaded NC (RGD+) which caused a significant reduction in VEGF concentration, the SFB-loaded NC (RGD−) treatment was comparable to free SFB. Furthermore, compared to non-targeted NC (RGD−) treatment, targeted NC (RGD+) treatment had quite a greater inhibitory effect on VEGF concentration, indicating that RGD conjugation could have an inhibitory effect on VEGF concentration. The VEGF concentrations in HUVECs treated with unloaded NC (RGD+) and NC (RGD−) were respectively 160.52 and 198.34 pg/mL, confirming a combined effect of RGD conjugation and SFB loading in GCRP-NC on VEGF concentration. Previous works have shown that αvβ3-blocking with an RGD motif can impair VEGF signaling in endothelial cells [56,60].

The anti-angiogenic activity of the synthesized nanocomposite was also evaluated in vivo using the CAM assay. The CAM assay is an angiogenesis study with several advantages such as ease of operation and low cost. Furthermore, the formation of a vascular network in a transparent matrix can be directly observed using stereo-microscopy in the CAM assay [61]. The CAM is initiated by poorly vascularization, during embryo growth, however, the angiogenesis process provides a rich vascular plexus [62]. The main duration of vessel development is until the 11th day of incubation [63]. The CAM treatment with anti-angiogenic formulations results in interferences with regular vascular growth. In this way, observing the anti-angiogenic effect is associated with the interference in the development of vessels in the CAM.

In this research, the surface of CAM was treated with PBS (negative control), NC (RGD+), NC (RGD−), SFB-loaded GCRP-NC (RGD+), and free SFB. After 3 days, the CAM was removed and photographed using a stereomicroscope. The CAM vessels were quantitatively analyzed by NIH ImageJ software using the angiogenesis analyzer plugin (Fig. 7 ). The total length of vessel network (Tot. Length), number of branching points (Nb. Branches), number of nods (Nb. Node), and number of junction points (Nb. Junctions) were all calculated. The control CAM sample (PBS-treated) showed normal neovascularization, with ordered and directional vascular patterns. CAM vessels in the treated groups exhibited different levels of irregularity with the reduced number of nodes, branches, and junctions. Compared to NC (RGD−)-treated CAM, the NC (RGD+) treatment showed more inhibitory effects on CAM neovascularization (P < 0.05). According to previous studies, the CAM vascular endothelium shows high levels of expression of αvβ3 integrin receptors to which RGD peptides can bind [64]. The angiogenesis was inhibited when the CAM was treated by RGD-peptides (cyclic RGD and ST1646 RGD peptide) or antibody antagonists of αvβ3 integrin receptor [65,66].

Compared to other treatment groups, the SFB-loaded GCRP-NC (RGD+) exhibited the greatest anti-angiogenesis effect on CAM neovascularization (P < 0.05). Tot. Length, Nb. Branches, Nb. Node, and Nb. Junctions were significantly reduced in the SFB-loaded GCRP-NC treatment group as compared to the free SFB. Functionalization of NC with RGD is in favor of recognizing αvβ3 integrin receptors on endothelial cells in the CAM. This recognition is also in favor of targeting NC and delivery of SFB to the endothelial cells. On the other hand, SFB can induce cytotoxicity and disrupt tube formation by HUVECs [67,68]. Moreover, SFB can inhibit migration, cell proliferation, and vascularization on the CAM [69]. Therefore, a combination of SFB with NC (RGD+) demonstrated a more effective anti-angiogenic activity compared to other groups. However, SFB has a significant effect on the efficacy of this system. All these findings indicated that targeted GCRP-NC (RGD+) could significantly increase cell internalization, resulting in improved anti-angiogenesis effects of SFB via receptor-mediated delivery.

In summary, an effective targeted delivery system was developed in this research for encapsulating a poorly water-soluble therapeutic agent (SFB) for imaging, tracking, and targeting angiogenesis. To improve SFB delivery to angiogenic endothelial cells, a targeted nanocomposite (GCRP-NC) was successfully prepared using GQDs and PLGA-NPs in a single multifunctional unit modified with RGD peptide. GCRP-NC combines the fascinating photoluminescence properties of GQDs, the biodegradability and biocompatibility of PLGA-NPs, and the specific recognition of αvβ3 integrin receptor by RGD peptide. The in vitro study revealed the significant capacity of GCRP-NC for loading SFB, with activity comparable to free drug. Furthermore, targeting moieties (RGD peptide) can be a practical strategy for enhanced cell internalization, particularly in endothelial cells expressing integrin receptors. Targeted GCRP-NC (RGD+) improved the in vivo anti-angiogenesis effects of SFB compared to non-targeted GCRP-NC, possibly due to increased cell internalization via receptor-mediated delivery. Thanks to these features, the proposed GCRP-NC (RGD+) can serve as a promising targeted delivery system for encapsulating poorly water-soluble therapeutic agents such as SFB, with the additional trackability and biocompatibility. Future studies can address SFB biodistribution and PK studies, as well as the targeted cancer therapy in combination with other chemotherapeutic agents.
Zahra Rahiminezhad: Investigation, Methodology, Visualization, Validation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing Original draft
Ali Mohammad Tamaddon: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Data curation, Supervision, Validation, Funding acquisition
Ali Dehshahri: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Validation
Sedigheh Borandeh: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Supervision
Samira Sadat Abolmaali: Validation, Supervision, Methodology
Hanieh Najafi: Methodology
Negar Azarpira: Validation, Conceptualization, Methodology

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bioadv.2022.212851.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Crit. Rev. Ther. Drug Carr. Syst.

[64] P.C. Brooks, R.A. Clark, D.A. Cheresh, Science, 264 (1994) 569-571.
[65] L. Belvisi, T. Riccioni, M. Marcellini, L. Vesci, I. Chiarucci, D. Efrati, D. Potenza, C. Scolastico, L. Manzoni, K. Lombardo, Mol. Cancer Ther., 4 (2005) 1670-1680.
